speaker: interviewer
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, cool. Good morning. My name is David Rottenberg. I have the pleasure of introducing survivor, Jacob Sandbrand. Today's date is January 22, 1997. This interview is being conducted in English in Rancho Bernardo, San Diego, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking. We're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Jack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I have the spelling of your complete name, first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack, J-A-C-K, Sandbrand, S-A-N-D-B-R-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, do you have a Hebrew name as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jacob?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that in, in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know how to spell it in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how about Jacob?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob, J-A-C-O-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and that's Jacob in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Jack, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kolomyja, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-L-O-M-Y-J-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: J-A. And that's in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland-- it was Poland, up to 1939, between World War I and World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a part of the Austria-Hungarian empire under Kaiser Franz Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after World War I--

speaker: interviewee
text: One.

speaker: interviewer
text: Became Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: '39, The Soviet army came in. And it became part of the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was an occupied territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they claimed that it was liberated territory.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born-- well, did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a two-year-old brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years older, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our father's name was Ephraim Zalman Zeichner.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had a different last name? He had a different last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Mena Hacker Sandbrand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Hacker was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her father's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Sandbrand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandbrand was her mother's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. What was the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Jack, tell me how was it or why it is that you use your mother's family name instead of your father's family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I was given to-- it was explained to me-- is that the government in 1925, the local people did not recognize my mother's marriage to my father, because they were married only by a rabbi. They had a ketubah. But they did not have a civil ceremony at city hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the marriage was considered by the Polish anti-Semitic government to be illegitimate. And when my father passed away and I was supposed to be registered for public school, the registration office would not accept my father's name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for, for me. And I had to register under my mother's maiden name. Is, is that why your mother also used her family name instead of Hacker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. This situation lasted for many, many years in that part of Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was part of the Galicia, Kolomyja?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kolomyja, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did-- was that true for your brother as well? Did he have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. His name was Nathan Sandbrand. And he was born, according to the birth certificate I just received, March 28, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years, two years older than you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was two years older, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when your father passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven days old. He died-- he passed away at my bris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances of his passing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during World War I, he was a youngster. He was too young to serve the Austrian army, which was-- Kolomyja was part of Austria in those days. And he came from a small town, which was about 20 kilometers away from Kolomyja.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called, just in [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Jablonuw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-B-L-O-N-O-- or N-U-W. And he went to visit his father in this village. And he was walking through the field and found a round object made out of steel with a hook on it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He figured-- in our living room, in our-- one of the rooms that we lived in at, at that time-- the family, actually, I wasn't there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a kerosene lamp, which was hanging on a chain. And it needed a counter weight so it could move up and down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he brought this object home. And it was laying there for a few months until before Passover. He picked it up, painted it up, and hung it up as the counterweight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover night, he came home from shul, from the synagogue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was standing at the table, saying kiddush. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they turned up the kerosene lamp. And that object heated up. And it exploded. Apparently, it was some kind of a bomb. A shrapnel went through his lungs. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] police came, the Polish police. And instead of sending him to the hospital, they arrested him for manufacturing a bomb. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish population of the town at the time figured it was an anti-Semitic act, because the, the house was lit up and the windows were wide open. And they thought somebody threw a bomb in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The investigation proved that what he said was true, you know, that he picked up the object in the field. And two months later, they released him from prison. He came home a day before my bris. And that day, he got up to open a barrel of beer. He had a hemorrhage. And he died. I was told that if he would have died a day before, I would have been named after him. But he died after my naming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, moving on, Jack. Let me ask you, what, what do you remember about Kolomyja? Was it a large city, a small city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a nice size city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- the population, I believe, was about 18,000 or 20,000. It was a county seat, Kolomyja. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- had-- we had a railroad going right through the middle of town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were many, many synagogues. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had one beautiful main synagogue. We had many well-to-do people, Jewish people. And we also had a large proportion of very, very poor people, Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish poor people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poor people that were struggling to make a living. We survived because mother had sisters in the United States. And they kept her once for a Jewish holiday. They used to send $10, or $15, or $20 to help. Their old parents, which they left many, many years before, and they never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that help your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had just enough to eat. Once in a Passover, they used to send us a package of old clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, Jack, how did your mother support you? Did she work? What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like everyone else in those days, used to buy a piece of cloth, and sell a piece of cloth, and make a few cents on it. But in later years, she organized a kindergarten type of a, like a, a babysitting service or Hebrew school. And she had about 15 to 20 children and preschoolers. And that helped her survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took care of other people's children while they worked, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: While those people worked, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you grew up with a lot of children around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a lot of those children, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like in Kolomyja? Was it an organized community? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an organized community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yeah. The religious were mainly the, the-- I'm looking for the word-- [LAUGHS] the organizers. But we also had the [NON-ENGLISH], which organized youth for Palestine, and the shomrim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had many, many Jewish organizations. They had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are we just discussing the Jewish part of it, or, in general, the whole city?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking about the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community-- they had-- what do you call it-- where they organized self-- where the richer part of the Jewish community was helping with a hospital, with an old age home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mind you, when the war started, I was only 14 in 1939. And as a youngster, I paid no-- I didn't pay too much attention to that. But in those days, the older people lived with the younger ones until we die-- [LAUGHS] until they passed away, if they had a chance to, to live that long. And, and that was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about your home. What was, what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, grandfather used to get up very early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Grandma and Grandfather-- yes, the two of them, and also an aunt, my mother's sister, her husband and three kids. We lived all in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large a house was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had four rooms. My mother and I, and my grandfather, and grandmother, and our brother, my brother, lived on one side of the house. It was like it had a hallway in between. On one side, It had a old-fashioned kitchen with a oven on top. And it had one room where we slept, all slept in that one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the grandparents slept in the kitchen part. It was large enough to put a bed in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother and us two boys slept in the other room. Across the hall in one room was mother's sister, her husband, and three children. And the front room was rented out to a carpenter who used to produce, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make all kinds of wooden furniture, and also made the Jewish lions-- I just remembered that-- for the synagogue. You know those that adorned the Torah part-- on top of the Torah. He was a very skilled carpenter, an artist, I would call him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's, let's go through--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that helped us survive, that rental also helps--

speaker: interviewer
text: To survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Survive, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about your grandparents. Were they your maternal grandparents or your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My maternal grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were, what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Elay Hacker, H-A-C-K-E-R. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And his wife's name was Lifsha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lifsha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lifsha. I don't know how to spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother's sister's names and her husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother sister's name was Pepi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say P-E-P-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pepi?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she married a man by the name of Steinkohl-- you know, stone and coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-E-I-N-K-O-H-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pinchas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember any of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, their son, Mendel, was born in 1922. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] His sister, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Rachel, was born in 1921. And he had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another sister, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Rose. And she was born, I believe, in 1927 or '28.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you all lived together in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lived together in that house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother also had other children whom she took-- whom she took care of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she do her work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they apparently rented a gardens in, in the near vicinity with a little shack like. And that's where the children used to be entertained, and played with, and whatever was necessary for a set amount of hours. It wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was like a seven or eight-hour thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have electricity in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until 1938. They first installed one bulb in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have running water in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The water, we had to carry for about a kilometer away. They had one faucet like in the middle of a town. And everybody used to come with pails. And we used to carry the water home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did you have-- then you didn't have flushing toilets or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had outhouses. It was a little shack. And we used to go outside. And twice a year, a man used to come and empty it out with a barrel and a horse, real modern. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Well, as you grew up, Jack, tell me a little bit about your life. Did you have friends? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I went to school, the area where I went to school was mostly a Jewish area. We lived like in a isolated ghetto part, mostly poor Jewish people. And the school was run by the Polish government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30% or 40% of the students were Polish and Ukrainian. Actually, most Polish students and Ukrainian students were going to their own parochial, church-sponsored schools in those days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a predominantly Jewish school. But the curriculum was in Polish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And most of the teachers, I would say, were anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What makes you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- classes began in the morning with a religious-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everybody had to stand up and pray-- though I kept my mouth shut, I didn't say anything-- in a Catholic-Christian prayer, which I still remember. And so they were very rough, the way they treated to us as Jews with remarks, you know? And in general, you could feel the anti-Semitism. I could feel it as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there, were there any things that you experienced in the way of beatings or things like that that you were exposed to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was hit a few times with a long ruler, yelled at, screamed at.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about, how about your kids or the students, the Polish students, in the class?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a few encounters there, too. Well, you would expect children to fight in classes, but to be called continuously dirty Jew, [NON-ENGLISH] which means filthy, full of vermin Jew, you know? In class many times and as we left school, fights ensued.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your way home?

speaker: interviewee
text: On way home. I was ganged up on by some, some of the students. I hit back. And they complained that I hit them, you know? So we, we got into trouble because of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got into trouble, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was suspended. I was sent home to bring my mother. How-- and when my mother came, how dare I start a fight and hit, you know, other kids. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In looking back on it now, Jack, would you say that the quality of the education that you received from the Polish government was a reasonable one?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: They taught you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They taught us how to read and write Polish, mathematics. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was pretty good at it, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the war started. And we didn't have a chance to go anywhere else. Except my older brother did go to gymnasium, which was like a high school, I would say. But you had to pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you go in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I went to about seventh grade. And the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, in '39, I finished the fifth grade. Then the Russians came in, the Soviet Union. And I went two grades in their schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to a Russian school as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they take over the school system?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took over the public schools, but it was a completely different situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it change?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were able to speak Yiddish in class if you wanted to.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't do that before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had Jewish teachers that were actually teaching in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Secular subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certain subjects, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they secular subjects like mathematics and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. They were teaching about Sholem Aleichem. They were teaching about Paris, you know, and different Yiddish subjects.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Jack. We're going to pause here to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll continue on after we change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sir.
